draft.introduction: Luke 4:16–30 records Jesus’ inaugural sermon at Nazareth, where he reads from Isaiah and declares prophetic fulfillment. This passage establishes the programmatic mission of Jesus in Luke’s Gospel.

translation.text: And he came to Nazareth, where he had been brought up. And as was his custom, he went to the synagogue on the Sabbath day, and he stood up to read. And the scroll of the prophet Isaiah was given to him.
translation.original_text: καὶ ἦλθεν εἰς Ναζαρέτ οὗ ἦν τεθραμμένος · καὶ εἰσῆλθεν κατὰ τὸ εἰωθὸς αὐτῷ ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῶν σαββάτων εἰς τὴν συναγωγήν

translation.text: And he rolled up the scroll and gave it back to the attendant and sat down. And the eyes of all in the synagogue were fixed on him. And he began to say to them, ‘Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing.’
translation.original_text: καὶ πτύξας τὸ βιβλίον ἀποδοὺς τῷ ὑπηρέτῃ ἐκάθισεν

translator_notes.note: Jesus reads Isaiah 61:1–2 but stops mid-sentence, omitting “the day of vengeance of our God.” This selective reading is theologically significant: Luke’s Jesus announces liberation without retribution.
